query: What are the projected sea level rise impacts on Venice specifically, including flood frequency projections and MOSE barrier effectiveness under different IPCC scenarios?
synthesis:
Projected sea level rise (SLR) presents a significant long-term threat to Venice, driven by a combination of global eustatic rise and localized land subsidence. Between 1991 and 2020, the mean relative sea level (RSL) in Venice rose by 4.9 mm per year, a rate significantly higher than the 1872–2019 average of 2.5 mm per year [Sea level and temperature extremes in a regulated Lagoon of Venice - Frontiers in Climate (2023)](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/climate/articles/10.3389/fclim.2023.1330388/full), [Sea-level rise in Venice: historic and future trends (review article)](https://nhess.copernicus.org/articles/21/2643/2021/).

### Sea Level Rise Projections by IPCC Scenario
Future RSL projections vary based on Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSP) and Representative Concentration Pathways (RCP):
- **By 2100:** Projections for low-emission scenarios (RCP 2.6) range from 32 cm to 62 cm, while high-emission scenarios (RCP 8.5) range from 58 cm to 110 cm. A high-end scenario involving extreme ice-sheet melting could result in an RSL rise of approximately 180 cm [Sea-level rise in Venice: historic and future trends (review article)](https://nhess.copernicus.org/articles/21/2643/2021/).
- **By 2150:** Under the high-emission SSP 5-8.5 scenario, RSL could reach 1.53 meters [Multi-temporal relative sea level rise scenarios up to 2150 for the Venice lagoon](https://www.preventionweb.net/publication/multi-temporal-relative-sea-level-rise-scenarios-2150-venice-lagoon). Under these conditions, the joint effects of tides and atmospheric forcing could lead to temporary extreme water levels of up to 3.47 meters [Future sea-level rise scenarios: an example from the Venice Lagoon (Italy)](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2025EGUGA..2713617T/abstract).

### Flood Frequency and Land Impact
The frequency of 'acqua alta' (tides exceeding 1.1 m) and subsequent MOSE barrier closures will increase drastically. By 2050, under RCP 8.5, the barriers may need to be closed for more than 20% of the time during the peak flooding months of October to December [Sea level and temperature extremes in a regulated Lagoon of Venice - Frontiers in Climate (2023)](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/climate/articles/10.3389/fclim.2023.1330388/full). By 2150, between 112 km" (SSP 1-2.6) and 159 km" (SSP 5-8.5) of lagoon land area will be exposed to flooding, with up to 65% of the total lagoon area potentially submerged in the worst-case scenario [Future sea-level rise scenarios: an example from the Venice Lagoon (Italy)](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2025EGUGA..2713617T/abstract).

### MOSE Barrier Effectiveness and Limitations
While the MOSE system has proven effective in blocking incoming tides since its 2020 activation, its long-term viability is constrained by several factors:
- **Failure Thresholds:** Research indicates the system will fail to protect all urban settlements once RSL rise reaches +0.40 m. Beyond this point, the "wind setup effect"—where wind drives water accumulation within the closed lagoon—becomes unmanageable [How long the Mo.S.E. barriers will be effective in protecting all urban settlements within the Venice Lagoon? The wind setup constraint](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0378383921000831). Additionally, the system is designed for a maximum water level of 3 meters, which may be exceeded by extreme events by 2150 [Multi-temporal relative sea level rise scenarios up to 2150 for the Venice lagoon](https://www.preventionweb.net/publication/multi-temporal-relative-sea-level-rise-scenarios-2150-venice-lagoon).
- **Subsidence at Inlets:** While the city itself is relatively stable, the three inlets housing the MOSE barriers are sinking at rates up to 2.9 mm/year, complicating the barriers' durability [Future sea-level rise scenarios: an example from the Venice Lagoon (Italy)](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2025EGUGA..2713617T/abstract).
- **Ecological and Economic Costs:** Frequent closures disrupt shipping access to ports and severely reduce water exchange between the lagoon and the Adriatic Sea. This can lead to oxygen depletion, modified thermohaline regimes, and amplified marine heat waves within the lagoon [How long the Mo.S.E. barriers will be effective in protecting all urban settlements within the Venice Lagoon? The wind setup constraint](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0378383921000831), [Sea level and temperature extremes in a regulated Lagoon of Venice - Frontiers in Climate (2023)](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/climate/articles/10.3389/fclim.2023.1330388/full).

Data gaps remain regarding specific future human-induced subsidence rates and the long-term impacts of continuous barrier operation on the lagoon's morphodynamics and sediment transport [Sea-level rise in Venice: historic and future trends (review article)](https://nhess.copernicus.org/articles/21/2643/2021/), [Sea level and temperature extremes in a regulated Lagoon of Venice - Frontiers in Climate (2023)](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/climate/articles/10.3389/fclim.2023.1330388/full).

sources.title: Future sea-level rise scenarios: an example from the Venice Lagoon (Italy)
sources.content:
This research evaluates Relative Sea Level Rise (RSLR) in the Venice Lagoon using Vertical Land Movement (VLM) data and IPCC AR6 projections for the years 2050, 2100, and 2150.

### Key Findings on Subsidence and Sea Level Rise:
- **Subsidence Rates (VLM):** While the ancient city of Venice is described as 'almost stable,' the three inlets where the MoSE (Modulo Sperimentale Elettromeccanico) barriers are located are experiencing significant subsidence of up to 2.9 mm/yr (based on GNSS and InSAR data from 1996–2023).
- **Projection Scenarios:** The study incorporates IPCC AR6 scenarios: SSP1-2.6, SSP3-7.0, and SSP5-8.5.
- **Extreme Water Levels:** Under the 2150 RSLR projections, the joint effects of astronomical tides, seiches, and atmospheric forcing could cause temporary water levels to rise as high as 3.47 meters.

### Projected Flooding Impacts:
- **Land Area at Risk (by 2150):** 
  - SSP1-2.6 (low emission): 112 km² of land exposed to flooding.
  - SSP5-8.5 (high emission): 159 km² of land exposed to flooding.
- **Maximum Inundation:** In a scenario where water levels reach 3.47 m, up to 65% of the total lagoon land area may be flooded. 
- **Critical Areas:** Lowland areas like Chioggia are projected to have approximately 90% of their land covered by water under these conditions.

### MOSE Barrier Effectiveness:
- The study highlights that the projected water levels (up to 3.47 m by 2150) pose significant questions regarding the 'durability and effectiveness' of the MoSE artificial barrier. 
- The high subsidence rates (2.9 mm/yr) specifically at the MoSE inlet locations further complicate the long-term viability of the barrier system in protecting the lagoon from high tides and sea level rise.

sources.title: How long the Mo.S.E. barriers will be effective in protecting all urban settlements within the Venice Lagoon? The wind setup constraint
sources.content:
Key findings regarding the effectiveness of the Mo.S.E. (Modulo Sperimentale Elettromeccanico) barriers in Venice under sea level rise (SLR) scenarios:

- **Failure Threshold:** The Mo.S.E. system is projected to fail in controlling flood events once the relative sea level rise (RSLR) reaches **+0.40 m**. Beyond this level, the barriers will no longer be able to protect all urban settlements within the lagoon simultaneously.
- **Wind Setup Constraint:** A critical limitation identified is the 'wind setup effect' that occurs during lagoon closures. When the barriers are raised, wind-driven water accumulation within the closed lagoon can raise water levels at specific urban settlements, leading to flooding even if the sea gates are closed. This constraint becomes unmanageable starting at the +0.40 m RSLR mark.
- **Closure Frequency and Duration:** Climate change will rapidly increase both the duration and frequency of barrier closures. Projections indicate that wind setup will increasingly necessitate 'long and repeated closures.'
- **Management Challenges:** Future management will face significant challenges in balancing flood protection with economic and environmental needs. Frequent and prolonged closures will conflict with: 
  1. Port and industrial activities (shipping access).
  2. Conservation of the lagoon ecosystem (water exchange and oxygenation).
- **Optimized Strategy:** The study proposes a new management strategy designed to identify the shortest possible closure duration that still prevents flooding across all settlements, though this strategy still encounters the +0.40 m RSLR failure limit.
- **Contextual Data:** The analysis utilized the last 20-year-long tidal records to model future scenarios. The research aligns with IPCC reports highlighting that climate-driven sea level rise will exacerbate flooding frequency in the Venice Lagoon, one of the world's most vulnerable historical areas.

sources.title: The Effect of Sea Level Rise on the Operations of the MOSE Barrier in Venice
sources.content:
### Sea Level Rise (SLR) Projections and Impacts on Venice
*   **General SLR Context:** The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) projects that SLR will increase due to thermal expansion and land-based ice melting. Even if global temperatures stabilize, sea levels will continue to rise because oceans and ice sheets respond on timescales of decades to millennia.
*   **Venice Specifics:** Projections for the next 100 years predict faster SLR for Venice compared to historical rates. Venice is uniquely vulnerable due to its location in the lagoon, many historic buildings, and land subsidence (compaction of soft sediment layers like clay and silt). 
*   **Relative Sea-Level Rise (RSL):** Land subsidence is a significant part of RSL in Venice, exacerbated by vertical land movement (VLM) and past groundwater extraction.
*   **Historical Flood Extremes:**
    *   **November 1966:** Water levels reached **194 cm + ZMPS** (Zero Mareografico Punta della Salute).
    *   **November 2019:** Water levels reached **187 cm + ZMPS**, inundating St. Mark's Square and the Doge's Palace.

### MOSE Barrier Effectiveness and Operations
*   **System Overview:** The MOSE (Experimental Electromechanical Module) consists of **78 gates** across three lagoon inlets (Lido, Malamocco, and Chioggia). It was first used in **2020**.
*   **Operation:** The barrier remains open during normal conditions to allow tidal exchange and shipping. It closes when high water levels are forecast to prevent flooding in Venice.
*   **Impact of SLR on MOSE:** As sea levels rise, the barrier will be required to close more frequently. Increased closures threaten the lagoon's ecological balance (by halting tidal flow) and disrupt shipping traffic. Frequent operations lead to questions about the long-term functionality of the barrier system.

### Flood Frequency and Adaptation Scenarios
*   **Research Focus:** The thesis evaluates the economic feasibility of adaptation strategies using the Rosner framework, accounting for potential 'regrets' (monetary losses) under different SLR scenarios.
*   **Evaluated Adaptation Strategy:** The primary adaptation analyzed is **raising the entire city of Venice by 30 cm**. This is proposed to be achieved by **injecting seawater into a deep soil layer** underneath the city.
*   **Key Findings:**
    *   Analyses resulted in smaller expected 'regrets' when opting to adapt (raising the city) across all SLR scenarios compared to not adapting.
    *   The difference between 'adapt' and 'not adapt' regrets increases with higher levels of SLR.
    *   Raising the city by 30 cm is estimated to allow more time to evaluate long-term alternatives to the MOSE barrier as water levels continue to rise.
    *   The report advises that adaptations are necessary to keep the MOSE barrier functional and the city safe from high waters.

### Extreme Value Analysis (EVA) for Venice
*   **Non-stationarity:** Stationarity (the assumption that the distribution of extremes is constant over time) is no longer valid for Venice due to SLR. The study compares non-stationary GEV (Generalized Extreme Value) distributions and models where parameters (like the mean) are functions of time to estimate future design values.

sources.title: Sea level and temperature extremes in a regulated Lagoon of Venice - Frontiers in Climate (2023)
sources.content:
### Recent Sea Level Trends in Venice
*   **Observed Rise:** Between 1991 and 2020, the mean relative sea level in Venice rose at a rate of **4.9 mm per year**. This is attributed to the combined effects of eustacy (global sea level rise) and land subsidence.
*   **Comparison:** This rate is significantly higher than historical averages, highlighting the Mediterranean as a focal area for amplified climate-related hazards.

### Future Sea Level Rise Projections (to 2050)
*   **Scenarios:** The study utilizes IPCC 6th Assessment Report (AR6) projections for three Representative Concentration Pathways: **RCP 2.6, RCP 4.5, and RCP 8.5**.
*   **Methodology:** Projections for the year 2050 were applied using a 'pseudo-global-warming' approach to simulate the evolution of the lagoon's dynamics, including sea level and temperature extremes.

### Flood Frequency and MOSE Closure Projections
*   **Flooding Threshold:** 'Acqua alta' is defined as a high tide event exceeding **1.1 m** above the Punta della Salute datum (ZMPS).
*   **MOSE Activation:** The MoSE (Experimental Electromechanical Module) barriers are raised when forecasts exceed the 1.1 m threshold. Actual closure procedures typically begin when the sea level in Venice reaches **60 to 80 cm** to ensure protection before the peak.
*   **Projected Frequency:** The number of flood events and subsequent MOSE closures increases drastically with sea level rise. 
*   **Severe Scenario Impacts:** Under the most severe scenario (RCP 8.5) by 2050, the MoSE barriers will need to be closed for **more than 20% of the time** during the months of October, November, and December.
*   **Extended Closures:** In cases of consecutive flooding events, the system may remain closed continuously for several hours or even days.

### MOSE Barrier Effectiveness and Side Effects
*   **Effectiveness:** Numerical simulations and observations (e.g., December 2020 events) confirm the barriers effectively block incoming tides and prevent the city from flooding when activated. However, on December 8, 2020, a failure to activate the system due to forecast misinterpretation led to immediate flooding.
*   **Ecological and Physical Impacts:** 
    *   **Reduced Water Exchange:** Frequent closures significantly reduce the exchange of water between the lagoon and the open Adriatic Sea.
    *   **Temperature Extremes:** Closures exacerbate **marine cold spells (MCS)** and modify the thermohaline regime. 
    *   **Marine Heat Waves (MHW):** Future projections indicate the lagoon will amplify temperature changes; the intensity of MHWs in shallow tidal flats is expected to be **more than four times larger** than in the open sea.
    *   **Morphodynamics:** Increasing closure frequency threatens the natural morphodynamic processes of shoals and saltmarshes by limiting sediment transport.

sources.title: Figure Box 13.1.1 | Venice sea level rise (SLR) and coastal flooding
sources.content:
The IPCC AR6 WGII report (Chapter 13, Box 13.1.1) provides specific data and projections regarding Venice's sea level rise (SLR) and flooding vulnerabilities:

- **Historical Flood Frequency:** The data tracks the decadal frequency of floods exceeding the "safeguard level" in Venice's city centre, illustrating the historical evolution of relative and mean sea level (derived from Frederikse et al.).
- **Sea Level Rise Projections:** Projected relative SLR at the Venetian coast is based on IPCC scenarios, including:
    - **"Very Likely" Range:** 5th–95th percentile projections.
    - **"Likely" Range:** 17th–83rd percentile projections.
- **Critical Thresholds:** The report charts the timing when critical relative sea level thresholds will be reached. This timing is highly dependent on climate scenarios (SSPs) and confidence levels (citing Lionello, 2012; Umgiesser, 2020; Lionello et al., 2021a).
- **High-Impact Scenarios (SSP5–8.5):** Under the SSP5–8.5 scenario, the upper limit of the medium confidence range represents a "low-likelihood, high-impact storyline." This scenario accounts for low-confidence processes such as ice sheet instability, which could lead to significantly higher sea level rise than the median projections.
- **MOSE Barrier Infrastructure:** Venice is connected to the Adriatic Sea through three specific inlets (visible in Landsat imagery) where the MOSE mobile barriers are situated. The effectiveness of these barriers and the frequency of their closure are directly linked to whether sea level rise remains below or exceeds critical thresholds. The "safeguard level" mentioned refers to the threshold above which flooding occurs in the city center if barriers are not closed.
- **Geographic Context:** The lagoon and its three inlets are the primary conduits for tidal flooding, which the MOSE system is designed to mitigate by isolating the lagoon from the Adriatic during high-water events.

sources.title: Multi-temporal relative sea level rise scenarios up to 2150 for the Venice lagoon
sources.content:
This study analyzes future Relative Sea Level Rise (RSLR) in the Venice Lagoon up to the year 2150, integrating IPCC Sixth Assessment Report (AR6) climatic projections with geodetic measurements from GNSS and InSAR data. 

### Projected Sea Level Rise & Scenarios
- **Scenarios Used:** The study evaluates impacts under three Shared Socioeconomic Pathways: SSP1-2.6 (low emissions), SSP3-7.0 (medium-high), and SSP5-8.5 (high emissions).
- **Maximum Rise:** Under the high-emission SSP5-8.5 scenario, RSLR could reach up to **153 cm (1.53 meters) by the year 2150**.
- **Land Area Impact:** By 2150, up to **65% of the lagoon land** could be flooded under worst-case projections, threatening over **130 km²** of land.
- **Regional Factors:** Land subsidence is noted as highly variable across the lagoon, which intensifies flood risk near coastal and urban infrastructure.

### MOSE Barrier Effectiveness
- **Design Limit:** The MoSE (Modulo Sperimentale Elettromeccanico) flood barrier system is currently designed to withstand water levels up to **3 meters**.
- **Projected Failure:** The study indicates that the barriers may become insufficient by 2150. Specifically, they could be **submerged during extreme events**, potentially rendering the system ineffective for protecting the lagoon under the most severe climate scenarios.

### Flood Impacts & Locations
- **Critical Infrastructure at Risk:** The flooding scenarios specifically threaten Venice city, industrial zones, and the Venice airport.
- **Mapping:** High-resolution Digital Surface Models (DSMs) using LiDAR data were used to map these risks, showing that coastal and urban infrastructure are most vulnerable to the combination of global sea level rise and localized subsidence.
- **UNESCO Status:** The findings emphasize that the UNESCO World Heritage Site's preservation requires urgent infrastructure adaptation and climate mitigation due to the scale of projected land loss.

sources.title: Sea-level rise in Venice: historic and future trends (review article)
sources.content:
This review article synthesizes historical and projected sea-level rise (SLR) for Venice, focusing on relative sea-level (RSL) changes which include both climatic sea-level rise and vertical land movement (subsidence).

### Historical Trends and Observations
- **Average RSL Rise (1872–2019):** Approximately 2.5 mm/year over the last ~150 years.
- **Subsidence Contribution:** Vertical land movement (VLM) accounted for roughly half of the historical RSL rise in Venice. 
- **VLM-Corrected SLR Rate:** After removing subsidence effects, the average rate from 1872 to 2019 is 1.23 ± 0.13 mm/year.
- **Recent Acceleration (1993–2019):** Tide-gauge data (after subsidence removal) indicates a higher average rate of +2.76 ± 1.75 mm/year, though this remains more uncertain.
- **Geographic Constraints:** Satellite altimetry does not provide reliable sea-level data specifically within the Venice Lagoon; local changes depend heavily on the Adriatic and Mediterranean Seas.

### Future Projections for Venice (by 2100)
Projections for atmospherically corrected RSL rise in Venice by 2100 vary significantly by scenario:
- **RCP2.6 Scenario (Low Emission):** 32 cm to 62 cm.
- **RCP8.5 Scenario (High Emission):** 58 cm to 110 cm.
- **High-End Scenario (Extreme Melting):** A plausible but unlikely high-end scenario involving strong ice-sheet melting yields approximately 180 cm of RSL rise by 2100.
- **Vertical Land Motion:** While specific future projections for human-induced VLM are not currently available, historical evidence suggests they could significantly exacerbate climatically induced SLR.
- **Uncertainty Factors:** Drivers of water mass exchange through the Strait of Gibraltar and regional atmospheric/oceanic processes contribute to interannual and interdecadal variability with magnitudes similar to historical observations.

### Flood Frequency and Defense Effectiveness (MOSE)
- The paper notes that the **phenomenology of surges, extreme water levels, their prediction, and broader implications** for the historical city (which include flood frequency and the role of the MOSE barrier) are detailed in related studies within the same NHESS special issue, specifically citing **Lionello et al. (2020a, b)** and **Umgiesser et al. (2020)**. These papers discuss the linkage between RSL changes and the effectiveness of flood protection strategies.

query: What are the projected sea level rise impacts on Venice specifically, including flood frequency projections and MOSE barrier effectiveness under different IPCC scenarios?
synthesis: Venice is already highly exposed because relative sea level rise there combines global sea-level rise with local subsidence. A 2021 NHESS review states that relative sea level in Venice has been rising at about 2.5 mm/yr, caused roughly equally by land subsidence and mean sea-level rise, and that this rise is the main driver of the historical increase in flooding frequency rather than stronger storm meteorology. That review reports that exceedances of 120 cm increased from fewer than 2 events per decade in the first half of the 20th century to 40 events in 2010–2019, while exceedances of 110 cm increased from 4.2 events per decade to 95 events per decade over the same comparison periods [Venice flooding and sea level: past evolution, present issues, and future projections (introduction to the special issue)](https://nhess.copernicus.org/articles/21/2633/2021/).

sources.title: NHESS - Venice flooding and sea level: past evolution, present issues, and future projections (introduction to the special issue)
sources.content: Venetian Lagoon area about 550 km^2; connected to Adriatic through Lido, Malamocco, Chioggia inlets. Historical city sits low in middle of lagoon. RSL rise is key driver of increased flood frequency. Line 378: 2.5 mm/yr RSL trend in Venice caused approximately equally by land subsidence and mean sea level rise. Line 379: frequency of water-height maxima above 120 cm increased from less than two events per decade in first half of 20th century to 40 events in 2010–2019; for 110 cm threshold, from 4.2 events per decade to 95 events per decade. Line 386: projected RSL rise by end of 21st century about 30–110 cm, with wider 10–120 cm range accounting for subregional uncertainty; could exceed 180 cm in a plausible high-end scenario. Lines 390-395: projections shown for RCP2.6 and RCP8.5 plus high-end scenario; closure durations of MoSE linked to RSL thresholds. 3 weeks/year of closures unlikely before 2040s but virtually certain before 2100 even under RCP2.6. 2 months/year unlikely before late 2050s even under high-end/RCP8.5, but virtually certain by late 2080s under high emissions and about as likely as not before 2100 under low emissions. 6 months/year closure likely before 2100 under high-emission scenario, suggesting current defence strategy becomes inadequate and new actions would be needed.

sources.title: Frontiers | Sea level and temperature extremes in a regulated Lagoon of Venice
sources.content: Table 2 climate deltas for 2050 simulations: relative sea level increases of 0.15 m (TREND-2050), 0.17 m (RCP2.6-2050), 0.19 m (RCP4.5-2050), 0.22 m (RCP8.5-2050). Line 491: with 0.22 m mean sea-level rise in RCP8.5-2050, number of acqua alta events triplicates to 54 total, including 9 exceptional floods (peak sea level 1.4 m, flooding about 60% of Venice). Table 3: CTRL-2020 had 16 acqua alta events and 357 hours of MoSE closure (3% of year); TREND-2050 34 events and 917 closure hours (7%); RCP2.6-2050 36 events and 1,008 closure hours (8%); RCP4.5-2050 42 events and 1,097 closure hours (9%); RCP8.5-2050 54 events and 1,324 closure hours (11%). Simulations note increases mainly due to mean relative sea-level rise rather than worsened storm surge forcing because future storm changes were not included, and studies generally predict slight weakening of severe weather in Adriatic.

sources.title: NHESS - Developing a framework for the assessment of current and future flood risk in Venice, Italy
sources.content: Assesses current/future flood risk using November 12 2019 storm surge event under scenarios for reference year 2060 and different MOSE states. Two MOSE closure states considered: fully functioning barrier (“allclosed”) and partial failure with Lido inlet remaining open (“lidoopen”). Lines 518-519: a fully closed MOSE keeps peak flood level significantly below safety threshold of 1.10 m ZMPS for all scenarios tested. A partially closed barrier reduces flood peak by about 0.3 m for SLR0 and SLR1, but dampening diminishes for SLR2. For sea-level rise of 0.45 m, peak outside at Piattaforma would be 2.25 m ZMPS and peak at Punta della Salute 2.10 m ZMPS, implying damping effect reduced by half. Lines 525-527: SLR2 closure occurs about 24 h earlier relative to flood peak versus ~4 h for SLR0/SLR1; partially functioning MOSE significantly reduces expected average flood depth for 90% of buildings for SLR0 and SLR1, but in SLR2 increased sea level dominates over partial-closure dampening. Lines 579-581: partially functioning MOSE could reduce damage from a 12 Nov 2019-type event by 17% to 48% for SLR0 or SLR1 if individual protection levels stay unchanged; under pessimistic climate-change prognosis, damage in SLR2-lidoopen could increase by factor 1.08 to 3.44 relative to SLR0-allopen; if household/business preparedness also worsens, damage could be up to 5.92 times higher.

sources.title: CLIMATE CHANGE | Possible sea level rise scenarios highlighted in Venice
sources.content: Summarizes INGV-led study 'Multi-Temporal Relative Sea Level Rise Scenarios up to 2150 for the Venice Lagoon (Italy)' using IPCC projections plus geodetic/topographic data. States MOSE is currently designed to protect Venice from high waters up to a 3 m difference between open sea and lagoon and a mean sea level of 60 cm in 2100; study says this design envelope could be exceeded toward the end of the century. Provides flood scenario outputs for 2050, 2100, and 2150 in absence of systems protecting from sea levels higher than today. Worst case in 2150: sea level during extreme high tide events could reach 3.47 m above Punta della Salute reference; potentially submerged territory could reach 139 km^2, extending to 226 km^2 (64% of investigated area) during exceptional high waters. Notes subsidence can reach up to 7 mm/yr in parts of lagoon, amplifying vulnerability.

sources.title: Attributing Venice Acqua Alta events to a changing climate and evaluating the efficacy of MoSE adaptation strategy | npj Climate and Atmospheric Science
sources.content: Study discusses Venice vulnerability to climate change and Acqua Alta events. Line 100 states sea levels in Venice have risen by approximately 26 cm over the past century. It reports projections indicating a change in extreme total water level between 20 and 40 cm for RCP4.5 (2100) and RCP8.5 (2050), and up to 80 cm for RCP8.5 (2100). Also identifies the location of MoSE barriers at three inlets and references prior studies on effects of barrier closure and implications under global sea-level rise.

sources.title: Sea Level Rise in Europe: Adaptation measures and decision-making principles
sources.content: Review article with Venice adaptation case study. Lines 425-426: after tests, MOSE became operational for first time on 3 October 2020 and in the first three winters operated 50 times, effectively protecting Venice from flooding, including severe events. Lines 427-429: closure of lagoon should be kept to a minimum for ecological and economic reasons; strategy also includes raising walkways to at least 110 cm above sea level. Lines 439-443: San Marco adaptation includes raising area to 110 cm and installing valves; temporary glass barrier protects St. Mark’s Basilica from minor acqua alta. Lines 445 onward: as sea level continues to rise, frequency of barrier closures will increase, posing management challenges for a regulated lagoon.

Sources like NHESS and Frontiers provide detailed projections and assessments specific to Venice, aligning well with the query's focus on IPCC scenarios and flood frequency.

However, while most sources offer relevant specifics, some details like exact IPCC scenario impacts are less emphasized, preventing a perfect score.

It also fails to integrate all relevant data from the sources, such as specific closure durations and flood frequency projections, leading to a partial and somewhat generic response.

query: What specific Total Allowable Catch quotas has ICCAT set for Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean bluefin tuna for each year from 2022 to 2026, and what were the scientific basis and stock assessment results behind each decision?
synthesis:
The International Commission for the Conservation of Atlantic Tunas (ICCAT) transitioned Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean bluefin tuna (E-BFT) management from a recovery plan to a multiannual management plan during the 2022–2026 period. The specific Total Allowable Catch (TAC) quotas and their scientific foundations are as follows:

### Annual TAC Quotas (2022–2026)
- **2022:** The TAC was set at **36,000 tonnes** under Recommendation 19-04 [REPORT OF THE 2022 ICCAT EASTERN ATLANTIC AND MEDITERRANEAN BLUEFIN TUNA STOCK ASSESSMENT MEETING](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/CVSP/CV079_2022/n_3/CV079030426.pdf), [ICCAT Executive Summary: East Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna (BFT-E)](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/SCRS/ExecSum/BFT_E_ENG.pdf).
- **2023–2025:** The TAC was increased to **40,570 tonnes** per year following Recommendation 22-08 [REPORT OF THE 2022 ICCAT EASTERN ATLANTIC AND MEDITERRANEAN BLUEFIN TUNA STOCK ASSESSMENT MEETING](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/CVSP/CV079_2022/n_3/CV079030426.pdf), [ICCAT Executive Summary: East Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna (BFT-E)](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/SCRS/ExecSum/BFT_E_ENG.pdf).
- **2026:** The TAC was further increased to **48,403 tonnes**, representing a 19.3% increase from the previous management cycle [Bluefin Tuna Quota Increase Approved at ICCAT - On The Water](https://onthewater.com/news/2025/12/01/iccat-new-bluefin-and-shark-rules-2026), [ICCAT Recommendation Establishing a Multi-annual Management Plan for Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna (Rec. 25-04)](https://iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2025-04-e.pdf).

### Scientific Basis and Stock Assessment Results
- **2022 Stock Assessment:** Decisions for the 2023–2025 period were based on a 2022 assessment using three models: Virtual Population Analysis (VPA), Stock Synthesis (SS), and the Age-Structured Assessment Programme (ASAP) [REPORT OF THE 2022 ICCAT EASTERN ATLANTIC AND MEDITERRANEAN BLUEFIN TUNA STOCK ASSESSMENT MEETING](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/CVSP/CV079_2022/n_3/CV079030426.pdf). 
    - **Spawning Stock Biomass (SSB):** All models showed a strong increase in SSB since the late 2000s [REPORT OF THE 2022 ICCAT EASTERN ATLANTIC AND MEDITERRANEAN BLUEFIN TUNA STOCK ASSESSMENT MEETING](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/CVSP/CV079_2022/n_3/CV079030426.pdf).
    - **Fishing Mortality:** Results varied by model. The VPA suggested overfishing was occurring (ratio of 1.16), while SS (0.72) and ASAP (0.542) indicated overfishing was not occurring. The median estimate was 0.81, leading to a status of 'not undergoing overfishing' [REPORT OF THE 2022 ICCAT EASTERN ATLANTIC AND MEDITERRANEAN BLUEFIN TUNA STOCK ASSESSMENT MEETING](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/CVSP/CV079_2022/n_3/CV079030426.pdf), [ICCAT Executive Summary: East Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna (BFT-E)](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/SCRS/ExecSum/BFT_E_ENG.pdf).
- **Management Procedure (MP) Adoption:** In 2022, ICCAT adopted a formal Management Procedure (Rec. 22-09) that uses a pre-agreed harvest control rule to set TACs based on five abundance indices, including Japanese longline CPUE and various aerial and larval surveys [ICCAT Executive Summary: East Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna (BFT-E)](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/SCRS/ExecSum/BFT_E_ENG.pdf).
- **2026 Decision Basis:** The 2026 quota was determined by applying the 'BR' (Butterworth-Rademeyer) component of the MP. In 2025, the Standing Committee on Research and Statistics (SCRS) evaluated whether 'exceptional circumstances' existed to deviate from the MP; since no consensus on such circumstances was reached, the standard MP formula was used to calculate the 48,403-tonne limit [ICCAT Recommendation Establishing a Multi-annual Management Plan for Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna (Rec. 25-04)](https://iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2025-04-e.pdf).

### Data Gaps and Uncertainties
While stock status has improved, the SCRS noted persistent uncertainties, including unquantified illegal, unreported, and unregulated (IUU) catches and 'inflated' historical catch data from 1998–2007 [REPORT OF THE 2022 ICCAT EASTERN ATLANTIC AND MEDITERRANEAN BLUEFIN TUNA STOCK ASSESSMENT MEETING](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/CVSP/CV079_2022/n_3/CV079030426.pdf), [ICCAT Executive Summary: East Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna (BFT-E)](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/SCRS/ExecSum/BFT_E_ENG.pdf). There are also gaps in size and catch-effort statistics for the Mediterranean prior to 2014 [ICCAT Executive Summary: East Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna (BFT-E)](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/SCRS/ExecSum/BFT_E_ENG.pdf).

sources.title: Bluefin Tuna Quota Increase Approved at ICCAT - On The Water
sources.content:
The International Commission for the Conservation of Atlantic Tunas (ICCAT) met in Seville, Spain, on November 24th, 2025, and established new catch limits and conservation measures for Atlantic tuna and shark fisheries from 2026 through 2028. 

### Bluefin Tuna Total Allowable Catch (TAC) Quotas:
- **Eastern Atlantic & Mediterranean Bluefin (2026–2028):** The TAC has been set at **48,403 metric tons**, which represents a **19.3% increase** from previous levels.
- **Western Atlantic Bluefin (2026–2028):** The TAC has been set at **3,081.6 metric tons**, representing a **13% increase**.

### Scientific Basis and Related Decisions:
- **Management Procedures:** For Western Atlantic skipjack, ICCAT adopted a formal management procedure for the first time. For bluefin tuna, the higher TACs indicate a decision based on the commission's management procedures, though specific biomass or stock assessment figures (such as SSB or mortality rates) are not explicitly detailed on this page.
- **Shark Conservation Measures:** 
  - **South Atlantic shortfin mako:** ICCAT implemented a strict **1,000-ton mortality cap** across all member countries.
  - **Basking shark and great white shark:** Adopted complete **no-retention rules**, prohibiting the keeping, landing, or transshipping of these species; any accidental catches must be released immediately.
- **Enforcement and Treaty Integration:** ICCAT passed a resolution to prepare for the upcoming **BBNJ (Biodiversity Beyond National Jurisdiction) high-seas treaty** and strengthened overall enforcement tools.
- **Impact on U.S. Allocations:** The international increase in bluefin quotas provides NOAA with more flexibility when adjusting U.S. bluefin allocations for the 2026–2028 period. Changes to recreational limits will be determined through future NOAA rulemaking.

sources.title: REPORT OF THE 2022 ICCAT EASTERN ATLANTIC AND MEDITERRANEAN BLUEFIN TUNA STOCK ASSESSMENT MEETING
sources.content:
The 2022 ICCAT stock assessment for Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean bluefin tuna (E-BFT) utilized three modeling platforms—Virtual Population Analysis (VPA), Stock Synthesis (SS), and the Age-Structured Assessment Programme (ASAP)—to determine the scientific basis for management decisions.

### Stock Assessment Results (Scientific Basis)
*   **Spawning Stock Biomass (SSB):** All three models showed a strong increase in SSB since the late 2000s. The Stock Synthesis model specifically indicated a sharp decline between 1950 and 1970, a stable low level until 2010, followed by a steady increase to 2020. 
*   **Recruitment:** Recent estimates indicate a clear increase, though the magnitude is uncertain. Strong recruitment years were identified in 1994 and 2003. Due to retrospective bias, recruitment estimates since 2011 were patched at the unfished equilibrium level (R0) for projections.
*   **Fishing Mortality and Stock Status (F_current/F_0.1):** The models provided a range of results regarding overfishing status relative to the F0.1 reference level:
    - **VPA:** Calculated a ratio of 1.16, indicating overfishing was occurring.
    - **Stock Synthesis:** Calculated a ratio of 0.72, indicating overfishing was not occurring. The terminal year (2020) harvest rate was estimated at approximately 7% of total biomass.
    - **ASAP:** Calculated a ratio of 0.542, also indicating no overfishing.
*   **Selectivity:** A substantial change in selectivity occurred in the late 1990s, shifting from a focus on young ages to full selection of older ages post-1999, which aligns with increased regulatory monitoring and control.
*   **Growth Assumptions:** The group adopted a Richards growth model with L_infinity fixed at 271 cm, based on data from large bluefin tuna caught by northern Canada handline and Norway purse seine fleets.

### Management Advice and TAC Quotas (2022–2026)
While the July 2022 technical report focused on the assessment results, the management advice for setting Total Allowable Catch (TAC) quotas was finalized during subsequent 2022 meetings based on these results and the Management Strategy Evaluation (MSE) framework.
*   **2022 TAC:** Set at 36,000 tonnes (under previous Recommendation 21-08).
*   **2023–2025 TAC:** Following the 2022 assessment and the adoption of a Management Procedure (MP), the TAC was set at 40,570 tonnes per year for 2023, 2024, and 2025 (Recommendation 22-08).
*   **2026 TAC:** The adopted Management Procedure (MP) is designed to determine the TAC for future cycles (typically 3-year periods) based on the scientific results of the MSE. Under the MP adopted in 2022, the 40,570 t quota serves as the baseline for the first management cycle (2023–2025), with the 2026 quota to be determined by the MP's harvest control rule.
*   **Catch Statistics:** The group noted that 'inflated catches' from 1998–2007 (estimated to account for unreported/illegal catches) remain the best available historical data for model scaling, as officially reported catches were considered absolute minimum estimates.

sources.title: ICCAT Executive Summary: East Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna (BFT-E)
sources.content:
### Total Allowable Catch (TAC) Quotas (2022–2026)
- **2023–2025:** The TAC for the Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean (BFT-E) is set at **40,570 t** per year (established under Recommendation 22-08).
- **2022:** While not explicitly listed as a single quota number in this summary's text, the reported catch for 2022 was **35,110 t**, which followed the stabilization of catches around previously established TAC levels (Rec. 19-04 set the 2022 TAC at 36,000 t). 
- **2026:** The TAC for 2026 is not yet specified in this document. The Management Procedure (MP) provides TAC advice that is typically set for multi-year periods; the next stock assessment is scheduled for **2026 or 2027**, which will inform future quotas.

### Scientific Basis and Management Procedure (MP)
- **Management Procedure Adoption:** In 2022, ICCAT adopted a Management Procedure (MP) for both western and eastern management areas (Rec. 22-09). This links the TACs of both areas under one framework and uses simulation-tested rules to achieve Maximum Sustainable Yield (MSY) objectives.
- **MP Indices:** The MP for the East uses five specific abundance indices: two CPUE (Catch Per Unit Effort) indices and three surveys. These include the Japanese longline CPUE (Northeast Atlantic), French aerial survey, and West Mediterranean Larval survey.
- **Exceptional Circumstances (EC):** The Committee evaluates updated abundance indices annually to determine if "exceptional circumstances" (Rec. 23-07) exist that would require deviating from the TAC. As of the 2024 reporting, no EC existed to warrant a change to the 40,570 t TAC for 2025.

### 2022 Stock Assessment Results
- **Models Used:** The assessment integrated results from three modeling platforms: Virtual Population Analysis (VPA), Stock Synthesis (SS), and Age-Structured Assessment Programme (ASAP).
- **Stock Status (as of 2020 data):** 
  - **Fishing Mortality:** The point estimate for FCURRENT/F0.1 is **0.81** (95% CI: 0.48–1.62). Since this is below 1.0, the stock is categorized as **not undergoing overfishing**.
  - **Spawning Stock Biomass (SSB):** SSB showed a progressive decline from the 1970s until the 2006 Recovery Plan. Since the late 2000s, there has been a **strong increase in SSB**, though the exact magnitude varies by model (ASAP shows the largest increase, VPA the lowest).
  - **Recruitment:** Two distinct periods were identified: low recruitment before 1990 and higher recruitment thereafter. Recent recruitment estimates remain highly uncertain.
  - **Fishing Mortality Trends:** A drastic decline in fishing mortality has occurred since the 2006 Recovery Plan (Rec. 06-05), although F has recently trended slightly upward.

### Uncertainties and Research
- **IUU Fishing:** The Committee noted ongoing, unquantified illegal, unreported, and unregulated (IUU) catches, which impede the ability to determine true stock productivity.
- **Data Quality:** Improvements have been made via stereo video cameras (since 2014) and the GBYP research program, but gaps remain in Mediterranean size and catch-effort statistics prior to 2014.
- **Biological Assumptions:** The Committee assumes BFT-E contributes fully to spawning at **age 5**. Natural mortality is based on a single age-specific vector adopted in 2017.

sources.title: ICCAT Recommendation Establishing a Multi-annual Management Plan for Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean Bluefin Tuna (Rec. 25-04)
sources.content:
### Total Allowable Catch (TAC) Quotas (2022–2026)
The provided document (Recommendation 25-04, replacing Rec. 24-05) focuses on the management plan implementation from 2023 onwards and explicitly sets the TAC for the 2026–2028 period.

- **2026 TAC:** Set at **48,403 t** (inclusive of dead discards). 
- **2023–2025 Period:** The document notes that a management plan started in 2023 based on the Management Procedure (MP) adopted in 2022 (Rec. 23-07 / 22-09). While this specific document does not list the 2022–2025 figures, it replaces Rec. 24-05 which governed the prior year.
- **2026–2028 Allocation Table (Total 48,403 t):**
  - **European Union:** 25,164.62 t
  - **Morocco:** 4,379.47 t
  - **Japan:** 3,559.41 t
  - **Tunisia:** 3,508.85 t
  - **Türkiye:** 3,094.45 t
  - **Libya:** 2,950.03 t
  - **Algeria:** 2,443.64 t (plus 17 t transfer from reserves)
  - **Egypt:** 549.00 t
  - **Norway:** 461.38 t
  - **Albania:** 457.37 t
  - **Korea:** 368.93 t
  - **China:** 286.15 t
  - **Iceland:** 253.14 t
  - **Syria:** 238.00 t
  - **United Kingdom:** 230.56 t
  - **Chinese Taipei:** 101.00 t
  - **Mauritania, Namibia, Panama:** 55.00 t each
  - **Research Reserve:** 20 t
  - **Unallocated Reserves:** 117 t (includes 100 t for longline bycatch and 17 t for Algeria).

### Scientific Basis and Stock Assessment Results
- **Scientific Advice (SCRS):** The Standing Committee on Research and Statistics (SCRS) advised moving from a recovery plan to a management plan because the stock status no longer requires the emergency measures introduced under the previous Recovery Plan (Rec. 17-07).
- **Management Procedure (MP):** The TAC decisions are based on the Management Procedure (MP) adopted at the 2022 Commission's Annual meeting. This MP is designed to manage the stock sustainably by using a pre-agreed formula (Decision Rule) to set catch limits based on scientific indicators.
- **2025 Stock Assessment/Review:** In 2025, the SCRS assessed the occurrence of "exceptional circumstances" (conditions where the MP might not be performing as expected or where the stock is outside the tested range). The committee was unable to reach a consensus on the existence of these circumstances. 
- **Decision Rule Application:** Because the occurrence of exceptional circumstances was not confirmed, the TAC for the eastern area was calculated using the **"BR" (Butterworth-Rademeyer)** component of the MP, resulting in the 48,403 t limit for 2026.
- **Capacity Management:** Fishing capacity must be commensurate with quotas. For purse-seiners, capacity is limited to a maximum variation of 20% compared to the 2018 baseline. Adjustments must use catch rates proposed by the SCRS in 2009, which are reviewed following each stock assessment.
- **Minimum Size:** Remains at 30 kg or 115 cm fork length, with specific derogations (8 kg / 75 cm) for certain small-scale fisheries (e.g., Azores baitboats, Adriatic farming, and Bay of Biscay French baitboats).

sources.title: Multiannual management plan for bluefin tuna in the eastern Atlantic and the Mediterranean - 2019/0272(COD)
sources.content:
The document provides a summary of the legislative procedure (2019/0272(COD)) for a multiannual management plan for bluefin tuna in the eastern Atlantic and the Mediterranean, dated July 24, 2023. 

Key details regarding catch management and regulations include:
- Implementation of International Commission for the Conservation of Atlantic Tunas (ICCAT) Recommendations: The proposal seeks to implement ICCAT Recommendation 18-02 and parts of ICCAT Recommendation 22-04 into European law to fulfill international obligations and provide legal certainty.
- Quota Allocation: Includes provisions for Member States to allocate sectoral quotas within their annual fishing plans, with specific mention of the framework for small-scale fishing.
- Fishing Opportunities: Covers rules for the allocation of fishing opportunities and the conditions for carrying over non-harvested live bluefin tuna from previous years' catches.
- Technical Rules: Establishes rules on the caging of bluefin tuna and the responsibility to launch investigations into irregularities concerning catches.
- Legislative Status: The Committee on Fisheries recommended the approval of the Council’s first reading position, which reflects an agreement between the European Parliament and the Council to adopt the regulation amending Regulations (EC) No 1936/2001, (EU) 2017/2107, and (EU) 2019/833, and repealing Regulation (EU) 2016/1627.

Note: This specific procedural summary does not list the individual Total Allowable Catch (TAC) tonnage figures for each year from 2022 to 2026, nor the detailed scientific stock assessment results (SCRS) behind the decisions; it focuses on the legislative implementation of the ICCAT recommendations (specifically 18-02 and 22-04) that contain those figures.

query: What specific Total Allowable Catch quotas has ICCAT set for Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean bluefin tuna for each year from 2022 to 2026, and what were the scientific basis and stock assessment results behind each decision?
synthesis:
Here are the **ICCAT Total Allowable Catch (TAC) quotas for Eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean bluefin tuna** for each year **2022–2026**, together with the **scientific basis and stock-assessment results** that ICCAT cited or used in making each decision.

## TACs by year

- **2022:** **36,000 t**. ICCAT’s 2022 eastern bluefin management measure states that the **first TACs derived from the new Management Procedure (MP) apply in 2023, 2024, and 2025**, which implies that **2022 remained under the pre-existing quota regime** rather than the new MP-based TACs. The 2022-adopted MP recommendation also states that the 2023–2025 TACs are the “first TACs derived from the MP,” confirming that **2022 was the last year before MP-derived TACs took effect**. [Recommendation 22-09](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-09-e.pdf) [Recommendation 22-08](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-08-e.pdf)
- **2023:** **40,570 t**. ICCAT set the TAC for **2023–2025 at 40,570 t inclusive of dead discards** in the eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean. [Recommendation 22-08](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-08-e.pdf)
- **2024:** **40,570 t**. The same ICCAT recommendation fixed the TAC for **2023, 2024, and 2025** at **40,570 t**. [Recommendation 22-08](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-08-e.pdf)
- **2025:** **40,570 t**. ICCAT again kept the TAC at **40,570 t** because the TAC was set as a **3-year constant management-cycle outcome** under the MP for **2023–2025**. [Recommendation 22-08](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-08-e.pdf) [Recommendation 22-09](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-09-e.pdf)
- **2026:** **48,403 t**. ICCAT announced after its **24 November 2025** annual meeting that the eastern bluefin TAC for the period **2026–2028** was set at **48,403 t**, a **19.3% increase** from the prior eastern TAC. [ICCAT Press Release, 24 Nov 2025](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Meetings/Docs/2025/Press_Release_2025_ENG.pdf)

## Scientific basis and stock-assessment results behind each year’s TAC decision

### 2022 TAC (36,000 t)
For **2022**, the sources retrieved here do **not include a 2021 or earlier ICCAT recommendation page explicitly showing the eastern TAC number for 2022**, but the later ICCAT and NOAA documents make clear that the **new bluefin MP only began generating TACs starting in 2023**, so the 2022 TAC was **not** produced by the MP. [Recommendation 22-09](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-09-e.pdf) [Recommendation 22-08](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-08-e.pdf)

The **scientific basis in 2022** was the **2022 eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean bluefin stock assessment**, which ICCAT’s bluefin MSE page identifies as a major input to the management process and as the most recent **full eastern stock assessment** before the MP-driven 2023–2025 TACs. The ICCAT MSE page states that full stock assessments were conducted for eastern bluefin in **2017, 2020, and 2022**, and that the MSE process was built around those assessment efforts. [ICCAT Bluefin MSE page](https://www.iccat.int/mse/en/specMSE_BFT.asp)

The **2023 SCRS report** summarizes what happened at that **2022 stock assessment meeting**: the Committee decided to use **ten abundance indices up to 2020** for the eastern stock assessment, consisting of **seven CPUE series and three fishery-independent indices**. The report also states that the **current MP uses five indices in each management area**, and in the East specifically uses **two CPUE indices and three surveys**. [SCRS 2023 Report](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Meetings/Docs/2023/REPORTS/2023_SCRS_ENG.pdf)

The same SCRS report says reported eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean catches for **2018–2022** were **27,782 t, 31,134 t, 35,038 t, 35,095 t, and 35,102 t**, respectively, and warns that **ongoing, unquantified IUU catches** remained a “serious impediment” to determining productivity and providing reliable biomass-based TAC advice. It also states that ICCAT continued to treat **1998–2007 realized catches as roughly 50,000–61,000 t per year** because historical catches had been seriously under-reported. That means the 2022 scientific picture was one of a rebuilt/improved stock but with **persistent uncertainty, especially from historical and ongoing IUU catch estimation**. [SCRS 2023 Report](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Meetings/Docs/2023/REPORTS/2023_SCRS_ENG.pdf)

**Bottom line for 2022:** the TAC still came from the **pre-MP quota framework**, while the **2022 stock assessment** provided the updated scientific foundation that would immediately feed into adoption of the new MP and the higher 2023–2025 TAC. [Recommendation 22-09](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-09-e.pdf) [ICCAT Bluefin MSE page](https://www.iccat.int/mse/en/specMSE_BFT.asp) [SCRS 2023 Report](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Meetings/Docs/2023/REPORTS/2023_SCRS_ENG.pdf)

### 2023 TAC (40,570 t)
The **2023 TAC of 40,570 t** was the **first eastern TAC generated under ICCAT’s newly adopted Atlantic bluefin Management Procedure**. Recommendation **22-09** says the MP was selected to calculate the TAC for both the western and eastern management areas, that the **first TACs derived from the MP apply in 2023, 2024, and 2025**, and that the management cycle length is **three years**. [Recommendation 22-09](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-09-e.pdf)

The MP’s explicit **management objectives** were: **(1)** stock status with at least a **60% probability** that both stocks occur in the **green quadrant of the Kobe plot**; **(2)** safety with **15% or less probability** that either stock falls below **BLIM**; **(3)** yield, to maximize overall catch; and **(4)** stability, limiting TAC changes between management periods to **no more than +20% or -35%**, with a tighter **phase-in cap of -10%** in the first cycle. The recommendation also states that **BLIM** for this MSE was set at **40% of dynamic SSBMSY**. [Recommendation 22-09](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-09-e.pdf)

The MP recommendation further says that if the TAC change resulting from the MP is **less than 1,000 t for the eastern management area**, the TAC is not changed; this was part of the built-in stability provisions. [Recommendation 22-09](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-09-e.pdf)

The NOAA summary of the 2022 ICCAT annual meeting states that, after application of the MP and its minimum-change provision, the **annual eastern Atlantic/Mediterranean bluefin tuna TAC increased 12.5% to 40,570 t** for **2023–2025**. [2023 International Agreements Book (NOAA)](https://www.fisheries.noaa.gov/s3/2025-03/2023-International-Agreements-Book-Rev-March-2025.pdf)

So the **scientific basis** for the **2023 TAC** was **not a single conventional assessment recommendation of “X tonnes”**, but rather the outcome of the newly adopted **MSE/MP framework**, which had been designed using stock-assessment information and tested against agreed performance metrics. ICCAT’s own MSE page says the MP was adopted in **2022** and TACs have been set **basically based on this MP** since then. [ICCAT Bluefin MSE page](https://www.iccat.int/mse/en/specMSE_BFT.asp)

### 2024 TAC (40,570 t)
The **2024 TAC** remained **40,570 t** because Recommendation **22-08** fixed the eastern TAC for the whole **2023–2025** management cycle at that level. [Recommendation 22-08](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-08-e.pdf)

The **scientific basis** for maintaining that number in **2024** was still the **same MP result from the 2022 decision cycle**, not a new annual stock assessment-triggered TAC reset. Recommendation **22-09** explicitly provides that the MP is applied on a **three-year cycle**, with those first TACs covering **2023–2025**. [Recommendation 22-09](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-09-e.pdf)

ICCAT’s MSE page also says the Commission adopted an **Exceptional Circumstances Protocol (ECP) in 2023**, indicating that in years between TAC resets, the SCRS monitors whether exceptional circumstances exist that might justify suspending or modifying normal MP application. [ICCAT Bluefin MSE page](https://www.iccat.int/mse/en/specMSE_BFT.asp)

No source retrieved here shows that ICCAT invoked exceptional circumstances for eastern bluefin in **2024**, so the most supportable conclusion from the retrieved sources is that the **2024 TAC simply remained the second year of the 2023–2025 MP-derived quota block**. [Recommendation 22-08](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-08-e.pdf) [Recommendation 22-09](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-09-e.pdf) [ICCAT Bluefin MSE page](https://www.iccat.int/mse/en/specMSE_BFT.asp)

### 2025 TAC (40,570 t)
The **2025 TAC** also remained **40,570 t**, again because it was the **third and final year** of the **2023–2025** TAC block established in Recommendation **22-08**. [Recommendation 22-08](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-08-e.pdf)

The scientific basis remained the same MP framework and underlying stock-assessment/MSE conditioning used when ICCAT adopted Recommendation **22-09**. That recommendation required the SCRS to run the selected MP according to the schedule in Annex 3 and advise the Commission of the resulting TACs for both management areas. [Recommendation 22-09](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-09-e.pdf)

The retrieved sources do **not** provide a separate 2025 eastern bluefin stock-assessment result that directly generated a new 2025-only TAC decision. Instead, the 2025 TAC was still the final year of the already-decided **2023–2025 MP output**. [Recommendation 22-08](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-08-e.pdf) [Recommendation 22-09](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-09-e.pdf)

### 2026 TAC (48,403 t)
For **2026**, ICCAT’s **24 November 2025** press release states that the Commission set a new eastern bluefin TAC of **48,403 t for 2026–2028**, corresponding to a **19.3% increase**. The same press release says these TACs were set **“based on the current management procedure.”** [ICCAT Press Release, 24 Nov 2025](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Meetings/Docs/2025/Press_Release_2025_ENG.pdf)

That means the **scientific basis** for the **2026 TAC** was again the **MP**, not a fresh full eastern stock assessment in 2025. The same ICCAT press release explicitly notes that in **2025** full scientific stock assessments were carried out for **bigeye tuna, white marlin, and Atlantic shortfin mako shark**—but it does **not** list eastern bluefin among the species assessed that year. [ICCAT Press Release, 24 Nov 2025](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Meetings/Docs/2025/Press_Release_2025_ENG.pdf)

So, based on the retrieved official sources, the **2026 TAC increase** appears to have come from the **scheduled rerun of the bluefin MP for the next three-year cycle**, using the current MP framework and its agreed objectives and stability rules, rather than from a new full eastern bluefin stock assessment in 2025. [ICCAT Press Release, 24 Nov 2025](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Meetings/Docs/2025/Press_Release_2025_ENG.pdf) [Recommendation 22-09](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-09-e.pdf)

## Concise year-by-year table

| Year | Eastern Atlantic & Mediterranean bluefin TAC | Immediate decision basis | Scientific basis / stock result behind decision |
|---|---:|---|---|
| 2022 | 36,000 t | Last year of pre-MP quota regime | 2022 full eastern stock assessment informed the transition to MP-based management; SCRS used 10 abundance indices up to 2020 and highlighted continuing IUU uncertainty. [Recommendation 22-09](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-09-e.pdf) [ICCAT Bluefin MSE page](https://www.iccat.int/mse/en/specMSE_BFT.asp) [SCRS 2023 Report](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Meetings/Docs/2023/REPORTS/2023_SCRS_ENG.pdf) |
| 2023 | 40,570 t | First MP output | MP adopted in 2022 with objectives tied to Kobe-green probability, BLIM risk, yield, and TAC stability; NOAA says eastern TAC rose 12.5% to 40,570 t. [Recommendation 22-08](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-08-e.pdf) [Recommendation 22-09](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-09-e.pdf) [2023 International Agreements Book (NOAA)](https://www.fisheries.noaa.gov/s3/2025-03/2023-International-Agreements-Book-Rev-March-2025.pdf) |
| 2024 | 40,570 t | Second year of 2023–2025 MP block | Same MP-derived 3-year TAC; no retrieved source indicates exceptional-circumstances override. [Recommendation 22-08](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-08-e.pdf) [Recommendation 22-09](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-09-e.pdf) |
| 2025 | 40,570 t | Third year of 2023–2025 MP block | Same MP-derived 3-year TAC; no retrieved source shows a separate 2025-only eastern stock-assessment TAC reset. [Recommendation 22-08](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-08-e.pdf) [Recommendation 22-09](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-09-e.pdf) |
| 2026 | 48,403 t | New MP output for 2026–2028 | ICCAT said TACs for 2026–2028 were set based on the current MP; 2025 full assessments were for other species, not eastern bluefin. [ICCAT Press Release, 24 Nov 2025](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Meetings/Docs/2025/Press_Release_2025_ENG.pdf) [Recommendation 22-09](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-09-e.pdf) |

## Important caveats

1. **The retrieved official ICCAT recommendation pages clearly support the 2023–2025 TAC of 40,570 t and the 2026 TAC of 48,403 t, but I did not retrieve an official ICCAT recommendation page in this search session that explicitly prints the eastern TAC number for 2022.** The 2022 figure of **36,000 t** is therefore included as the recognized pre-MP quota level, but in the retrieved set it is supported indirectly by later ICCAT/NOAA documents showing that 2023 was the first MP-derived TAC year and that the 2023 TAC represented a 12.5% increase. [Recommendation 22-09](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-09-e.pdf) [2023 International Agreements Book (NOAA)](https://www.fisheries.noaa.gov/s3/2025-03/2023-International-Agreements-Book-Rev-March-2025.pdf)
2. **The scientific rationale changes after 2022:** for 2022, the basis was still a conventional stock-assessment/update environment; for 2023 onward, the TACs were determined mainly by the **pre-agreed management procedure**, which itself was built from stock-assessment science and formal management objectives, rather than through annual ad hoc quota negotiation. [Recommendation 22-09](https://www.iccat.int/Documents/Recs/compendiopdf-e/2022-09-e.pdf) [ICCAT Bluefin MSE page](https://www.iccat.int/mse/en/specMSE_BFT.asp)

If you want, I can do a second pass focused specifically on retrieving the **exact ICCAT recommendation text for the 2022 eastern TAC** and, if available, the **full 2022 stock-assessment summary metrics** such as Kobe status probabilities, biomass/fishing mortality ratios, and candidate-model results.

sources.title: Recommendation 22-08: Recommendation by ICCAT amending the Recommendation 21-08 establishing a multi-annual management plan for bluefin tuna in the Eastern Atlantic and the Mediterranean
sources.content: Official ICCAT recommendation adopted in 2022. States that the management plan for eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean bluefin tuna starts in 2023 and is based on the Management Procedure in Rec. 22-09. Key TAC text at Part II, paragraph 4: 'The total allowable catches (TACs), inclusive of dead discards, for 2023 to 2025 shall be set at 40,570 t, in accordance with the MP. The TACs for 2026 and thereafter shall be decided at the 2025 Commission Annual Meeting in accordance with the MP.' The document includes the quota allocation table summing to TOTAL 40,570 t for 2023-2025. It also notes that Mauritania and Senegal may each catch up to 5 t for research, deducted from the unallocated reserve. The recommendation acknowledges SCRS advice to move from a recovery plan to a management plan because the stock status no longer appears to require emergency recovery-plan measures, and notes that the SCRS completed an MSE to establish a management procedure including harvest control rules.

sources.title: Recommendation 22-09: Recommendation by ICCAT establishing a management procedure for Atlantic bluefin tuna to be used for both the western Atlantic and eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean management areas
sources.content: Official ICCAT recommendation establishing the Atlantic bluefin tuna Management Procedure (MP). States that the MP shall be used to calculate TAC for both western and eastern management areas. Paragraph 2 sets management objectives: stock status objective is at least 60% probability both stocks occur in the Kobe green quadrant; safety objective is 15% or less probability either stock falls below BLIM; yield objective is to maximize overall catch; stability objective is TAC changes between periods no more than +20% or -35%, with a first-cycle phase-in where decreases are limited to 10%. Paragraph 4 states: 'The first TACs derived from the MP shall apply in 2023, 2024, and 2025. The management cycle length shall be three years; therefore, the MP shall be applied every three years.' Paragraph 6 states that if TAC change is less than 50 t for the western area and 1,000 t for the eastern area, the TAC shall not be changed. Footnote defines BLIM as 40% of the dynamic spawning stock biomass at MSY. Annex 1 lists performance measures including probability of being in the Kobe green quadrant, probability of overfishing, biomass ratios, lowest depletion relative to BLIM, median TAC over years 1-10 and 1-30, and TAC variation between management cycles.

sources.title: 2023 International Agreements Book (NOAA Fisheries, revised March 2025)
sources.content: NOAA summary of international fisheries arrangements. In discussion of Atlantic bluefin tuna, it states that the bluefin management procedure recommendation (Rec. 20-09 is apparently a typo for 22-09 in context) establishes TACs starting in 2023. It says: 'The TACs resulting from application of the MP, including application of a 50 t minimum TAC change provision for the west and 1000 t for the east, for each year of the 2023-2025 period are as follows: the annual western Atlantic bluefin TAC remains at the 2022 level of 2,726 t; and the annual eastern Atlantic/Mediterranean bluefin tuna TAC increased 12.5% to 40,570 t.' This provides explicit confirmation that the eastern TAC rose 12.5% for 2023-2025 and that 2023 was the first MP-derived period.

sources.title: ICCAT Atlantic Bluefin Tuna MSE page
sources.content: ICCAT’s dedicated page on the Atlantic bluefin tuna Management Strategy Evaluation. States that Atlantic bluefin has two stocks with long-distance movements and mixed-stock fisheries. Notes that MSE for Atlantic bluefin has been under development since at least 2014. States that a Management Procedure (MP) was adopted in 2022 (Rec. 22-09) and that TACs for both stocks have been set basically based on this MP since 2022. Notes that the Commission adopted the Exceptional Circumstances Protocol in 2023. The page also summarizes assessment/MSE history, stating that substantial SCRS work included full stock assessments for eastern and western Atlantic bluefin in 2017 and 2020, plus eastern assessment in 2022 and western assessment in 2021. The references section lists 2022 stock synthesis preliminary results for eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean bluefin tuna and multiple technical MSE papers.

sources.title: SCRS 2023 Report
sources.content: ICCAT Standing Committee on Research and Statistics report. The retrieved excerpt for eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean bluefin tuna states that reported catches between 2018 and 2022 were 27,782 t, 31,134 t, 35,038 t, 35,095 t, and 35,102 t, respectively; Mediterranean catches for those years were 19,624 t, 22,090 t, 24,164 t, 24,786 t, and 24,625 t. The report says ongoing, unquantified IUU catches are a serious impediment to determining productivity and providing reliable TAC advice. It also says historical eastern catches in the mid-1990s through 2007 were seriously under-reported, and realized catches for 1998-2007 were likely 50,000 to 61,000 t per year; since the 2017 stock assessment, these estimates have been treated as actual catches. The report further states that during the 2022 stock assessment meeting, the decision was made to use ten abundance indices up to 2020, comprising seven CPUE series and three fisheries-independent indices. It notes that the current MP uses five indices in each management area, and in the East specifically uses two CPUE indices and three surveys.

sources.title: ICCAT Press Release, 24 November 2025
sources.content: Official ICCAT press release from the closure of the 29th Regular Meeting of the Commission in Seville, 24 November 2025. States that ICCAT agreed on new TACs and quotas for bluefin tuna for the period 2026-2028 based on the current management procedures. Specifically says: 'The TACs have been set for the period 2026-2028 at 3,081.6 t and 48,403 t, for the western and eastern Atlantic stocks, corresponding to TAC increases of 13% and 19.3%, respectively.' It also states that in 2025, full scientific stock assessments were carried out for three species: bigeye tuna, white marlin, and Atlantic shortfin mako shark. The release lists adopted measures including a recommendation amending Recommendation 24-05 establishing a multi-annual management plan for bluefin tuna in the eastern Atlantic and the Mediterranean.
