query: What is the current deforestation rate in the Brazilian Cerrado biome, what are the main commodity drivers, and what specific enforcement actions has IBAMA taken in the last two years?
synthesis:
The Brazilian Cerrado has experienced a notable decline in deforestation over the last two years, following a period of sustained increases. For the most recent monitoring period (August 2024 to July 2025), the official deforestation rate was recorded at 7,235.27 km², representing an 11.49% decrease from the previous year [In 2025, deforestation fell by 11.08 percent in the Amazon and by 11.49 percent in the Cerrado](https://www.gov.br/secom/en/latest-news/2025/10/in-2025-deforestation-fell-by-11-08-percent-in-the-amazon-and-by-11-49-percent-in-the-cerrado). This follows a 25.7% reduction recorded between 2023 and 2024 [In one year, deforestation and conversion falls 30.6% in the Amazon and 25.7% in the Cerrado | WWF Brasil](https://www.wwf.org.br/en/?90200/In-one-year-deforestation-falls-306-in-the-Amazon-and-257-in-the-Cerrado). Despite this downward trend, the Cerrado remains Brazil's most deforested biome, with losses in 2024 exceeding those of the Amazon [Deforestation for soy continues in Brazilian Cerrado despite EUDR looming](https://news.mongabay.com/2025/10/deforestation-for-soy-continues-in-brazilian-cerrado-despite-eudr-looming/).

### Main Commodity Drivers
Deforestation is heavily concentrated in the "Matopiba" region (comprising the states of Maranhão, Tocantins, Piauí, and Bahia), which accounted for approximately 75% to 78% of all clearing in the biome between 2024 and 2025 [In 2025, deforestation fell by 11.08 percent in the Amazon and by 11.49 percent in the Cerrado](https://www.gov.br/secom/en/latest-news/2025/10/in-2025-deforestation-fell-by-11-08-percent-in-the-amazon-and-by-11-49-percent-in-the-cerrado). The primary drivers identified include:
- **Cattle Ranching:** Cited as the largest direct driver of deforestation in Brazil [Deforestation in Brazil's Cerrado reduces soy production and threatens supply chains (Zero Carbon Analytics, November 2025)](https://zerocarbon-analytics.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/11/2025-11-15-ZCA-Deforestation-in-Brazils-Cerrado-reduces-soy-production-and-threatens-supply-chains.pdf).
- **Soy Production:** Identified as the second-largest driver, with expansion linked to global traders like Cargill and Bunge [Deforestation for soy continues in Brazilian Cerrado despite EUDR looming](https://news.mongabay.com/2025/10/deforestation-for-soy-continues-in-brazilian-cerrado-despite-eudr-looming/).
- **Monoculture and Biofuels:** Other crops such as corn and cotton, as well as land clearing for biofuel production, have also been linked to illegal activities in the biome [Connecting Brazil’s Financial Market to Environmental Violations: The Hidden Impacts of Agribusiness on Indigenous Lands – Global Investigative Journalism Network](https://gijn.org/stories/connecting-brazil-financial-market-environmental-violations/).

### IBAMA Enforcement Actions (2023–2025)
IBAMA has significantly ramped up enforcement operations compared to the 2020–2022 period. Specific actions include:
- **Operation Caixa-Forte (March 2025):** A major initiative targeting the financial sector for granting rural credit to properties under environmental embargo. Three banks in the Matopiba region were fined more than R$ 3.63 million [The Liability of Financial Institutions in Brazil for Environmental Violations: Reflections from Operation Caixa-Forte](https://www.felsberg.com.br/en/the-liability-of-financial-institutions-in-brazil-for-environmental-violations-reflections-from-operation-caixa-forte/).
- **Increased Penalties:** Between 2023 and 2025, IBAMA saw a 130% increase in the value and number of fines issued, a 38% increase in the number of embargoes, and a 24% increase in flora-related violation notices in the Cerrado [In 2025, deforestation fell by 11.08 percent in the Amazon and by 11.49 percent in the Cerrado](https://www.gov.br/secom/en/latest-news/2025/10/in-2025-deforestation-fell-by-11-08-percent-in-the-amazon-and-by-11-49-percent-in-the-cerrado).
- **Field Operations:** Between August 2024 and July 2025, IBAMA and ICMBio conducted 91 specific operations in the Cerrado, resulting in 402 violation notices and 218 embargoes [In 2025, deforestation fell by 11.08 percent in the Amazon and by 11.49 percent in the Cerrado](https://www.gov.br/secom/en/latest-news/2025/10/in-2025-deforestation-fell-by-11-08-percent-in-the-amazon-and-by-11-49-percent-in-the-cerrado).
- **Strategic Policy:** The implementation of the Action Plan for the Prevention and Control of Deforestation in the Cerrado (PPCerrado) in late 2023 has centralized these efforts [In one year, deforestation and conversion falls 30.6% in the Amazon and 25.7% in the Cerrado | WWF Brasil](https://www.wwf.org.br/en/?90200/In-one-year-deforestation-falls-306-in-the-Amazon-and-257-in-the-Cerrado).

Data gaps remain regarding the long-term effectiveness of the European Union’s Anti-Deforestation Regulation (EUDR), as current technical definitions may exclude up to 74% of the Cerrado's "non-forest" native vegetation from its protections [Deforestation for soy continues in Brazilian Cerrado despite EUDR looming](https://news.mongabay.com/2025/10/deforestation-for-soy-continues-in-brazilian-cerrado-despite-eudr-looming/).

sources.title: In 2025, deforestation fell by 11.08 percent in the Amazon and by 11.49 percent in the Cerrado
sources.content:
### Current Deforestation Rate in the Cerrado
- **Rate:** The official deforestation rate for the Cerrado biome was **7,235.27 km²** for the period between August 2024 and July 2025.
- **Trend:** This represents an **11.49% decrease** compared to the previous period (August 2023 to July 2024). This marks the second consecutive year of decline for the biome, following five years of increases from 2019 to 2023.
- **Regional Concentration:** Approximately **77.9% of Cerrado deforestation** occurred in the "Matopiba" region, which comprises the states of Maranhão, Tocantins, Piauí, and Bahia.

### Main Commodity Drivers
While the article does not explicitly name specific commodities (like soy or beef) by name, it identifies the **Matopiba region** as the primary area of deforestation, accounting for nearly 78% of the loss. This region is internationally recognized as Brazil's main agricultural frontier for **soy and cattle production**. The government has implemented specific "Action Plans for the Prevention and Control of Deforestation" for the Cerrado to address these pressures.

### IBAMA Enforcement Actions (2023–2025)
IBAMA (Brazilian Institute of the Environment and Renewable Natural Resources) has significantly increased enforcement activities over the last two years (comparing the 2023–2025 period to 2020–2022):
- **In the Cerrado:**
  - **Violation Notices:** 24% increase in notices related to flora.
  - **Fines:** 130% increase in the value/number of fines issued.
  - **Embargoes:** 38% increase in the number of embargoes.
  - **Embargoed Area:** 26% expansion in the total area under environmental embargo.
- **In the Amazon:**
  - **Violation Notices:** 81% increase.
  - **Fines:** 63% increase.
  - **Embargoes:** 51% increase, with a 49% rise in total embargoed area.
- **Operational Specifics (Aug 2024 – July 2025):** Combined efforts by IBAMA and ICMBio included 91 specific operations in the Cerrado, resulting in 402 violation notices and 218 embargoes. In the Amazon, 312 enforcement actions led to 1,301 violation notices and 816 embargoes in federal conservation units.
- **Strategic Methods:** Use of advanced satellite monitoring (DETER and PRODES systems from INPE), training of specialized inspectors, and the rigorous removal of illegal occupiers from federal lands. 
- **Financial Support:** Approval of BRL 850 million from the Amazon Fund specifically to strengthen environmental enforcement actions for the control of illegal deforestation.

sources.title: In one year, deforestation and conversion falls 30.6% in the Amazon and 25.7% in the Cerrado | WWF Brasil
sources.content:
### Deforestation Rates in the Brazilian Cerrado
- **Current Rate (2023-2024):** The deforestation and conversion rate in the Cerrado was 8,174 km² for the period between August 2023 and July 2024. 
- **Trend:** This represents a 25.7% reduction compared to the previous period (August 2022 - July 2023). This is the first time deforestation has fallen in the Cerrado since 2019.
- **Leading States in Deforestation:** 
  - Maranhão: 2,487 km²
  - Tocantins: 2,019 km²
  - Piauí: 1,014 km²
  - These states, along with Bahia (which saw a 63.3% reduction), comprise the Matopiba region.

### Main Commodity Drivers
- **Agricultural Expansion:** The destruction is heavily concentrated in the "Matopiba" region (Maranhão, Tocantins, Piauí, and Bahia), which is described as the country's newest agricultural frontier.
- **Commodity Production:** The report explicitly links the high levels of devastation to the expansion of commodity production.
- **Land Type:** Unlike the Amazon, deforestation in the Cerrado is primarily concentrated in private areas, facilitated by more permissive legislation regarding land clearing.
- **Soy:** While the Soy Moratorium is mentioned as an effective tool in the Amazon, there is concern about the advance of destruction in the Cerrado where half of the natural vegetation has already been lost.

### Enforcement Actions and Policy Initiatives (Last Two Years)
- **PPCerrado:** The federal government released the "Action Plan for the Prevention and Control of Deforestation in the Cerrado" (PPCerrado) in late 2023. This initiative is currently being implemented to address the biome's critical situation.
- **Pact for the Cerrado:** Signed on November 6, 2024, by the Ministry of the Environment and Climate Change (MMA) to coordinate preservation efforts.
- **Federal Secretariat for Deforestation Control:** A specific federal secretariat was created in 2023 to lead efforts in controlling forest destruction.
- **Resumption of Control Plans:** Since 2023, there has been a resumption of the fight against deforestation and the reactivation of environmental control and governance bodies that were previously dismantled between 2019 and 2022. This includes the resumption of the Amazon Deforestation Prevention and Control Plan (PPCDAM), which serves as a model for Cerrado actions.
- **Monitoring and Monitoring:** Strengthening monitoring systems (such as PRODES-INPE) is cited as essential to the recent reduction in rates. Efforts have shifted toward integrated policies for both the Amazon and Cerrado due to their connectivity in water and energy security.

sources.title: Deforestation for soy continues in Brazilian Cerrado despite EUDR looming
sources.content:
### Cerrado Deforestation Rates
* **2024 Statistics:** The Cerrado lost 652,197 hectares (1.61 million acres) of native vegetation in 2024, making it the most deforested biome in Brazil for the second consecutive year, surpassing the Amazon.
* **Recent Clearance (2024-2025):** Two major producers, Mizote Group and Franciosi Agro, cleared 986 hectares (2,436 acres) between May 2024 and late 2025. Between January 2021 and April 2024, these same groups cleared 23,164 hectares (57,239 acres) in western Bahia.
* **Historical Trend:** Agricultural activity in the Cerrado expanded by 74% between 1985 and 2024.

### Main Commodity Drivers
* **Soy Production:** Agricultural expansion for soy is the primary driver. Major producers (Mizote Group, Franciosi Agro, Horita Group) supply soy to global traders Cargill and Bunge, who handle approximately 95% of soy exports from western Bahia to the EU.
* **Matopiba Region:** The agricultural frontier consisting of Maranhão, Tocantins, Piauí, and Bahia accounted for 75% of all Cerrado deforestation in 2024.
* **Supply Chain Links:** Deforestation is linked to soy used in chicken feed for major European retailers and fast-food chains like McDonald’s.

### Enforcement and Regulation
* **Legality of Clearing:** For the 23,164 hectares cleared by Mizote and Franciosi between 2021-2024, investigators could not identify deforestation permits for 60% of the area, indicating significant illegal activity. 
* **State Agencies:** While the article notes that authorizations were granted by Inema (the Bahia state environmental agency) for some recent clearing, it identifies a lack of enforcement and the potential for "greenwashing" through certification schemes like the Roundtable on Responsible Soy (RTRS).
* **EUDR Impact:** The European Union’s antideforestation regulation (EUDR) targets deforestation after 2020 but currently excludes roughly 74% of the Cerrado due to technical definitions of "forest," though some recently cleared areas in Bahia do meet the criteria.
* **Note on IBAMA:** The provided text does not list specific IBAMA enforcement actions (such as fine totals or named operations) from the last two years, focusing instead on private sector investigations by Earthsight and state-level environmental authorizations.

sources.title: Deforestation in Brazil's Cerrado reduces soy production and threatens supply chains (Zero Carbon Analytics, November 2025)
sources.content:
### Cerrado Deforestation Rates
*   **Recent Figures:** In 2023, deforestation in the Cerrado reached 1.1 million hectares, more than twice the rate recorded in the Amazon for the same year.
*   **Historical Impact:** At least 46% of the Cerrado’s native vegetation has been cleared for pasture or crops. In the Matopiba agricultural frontier (Maranhão, Tocantins, Piauí, and Bahia), the soybean area increased by 253% between 2000 and 2014, leading to the loss of 50% of that region's native vegetation.
*   **Trends:** Recent trends (2013–2022) show an average clearing rate of 361.2 kilohectares per year, while the historical average from 2008 to 2022 was 435 kilohectares per year. While rates have eased slightly, deforestation continues.

### Main Commodity Drivers
*   **Cattle Ranching:** Identified as the largest direct driver of deforestation in Brazil.
*   **Soy:** The second-largest direct driver of deforestation. Soy accounted for 14.5% of Brazil's total exports between January and September 2025. The report highlights a paradox where clearing for soy is profitable for individual farmers (who gain ~300 tonnes from 100 new hectares) but economically destructive at the regional level due to climate spillovers.

### Economic and Productivity Impacts
*   **Production Loss:** If no land had been cleared for soy in the Cerrado since 2008, the region would have produced an additional USD 9.4 billion in soy between 2013 and 2023 (an 8% output increase).
*   **Productivity Ratio:** For every one tonne of soy produced on newly cleared land, three tonnes were lost from existing fields due to climate-driven drying and heat stress.
*   **Climate Sensitivity:** Clearing 1,000 hectares causes a loss of 14,627 tonnes of soy in wet years (due to the loss of forested areas' ability to capture and store rainfall) compared to 1,213 tonnes in dry years.

### Enforcement and Governance
*   **IBAMA Actions:** The provided document does not list specific IBAMA enforcement actions from the last two years. It focuses on the economic and supply chain impacts of deforestation. 
*   **Policy Gaps:** The report notes the Cerrado has been largely "overlooked in environmental policies" compared to the Amazon. It mentions the Amazon Soy Moratorium (avoiding purchases from areas deforested after 2008) and the EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR), which prohibits imports from regions deforested since 2020. 
*   **Supply Chain Exposure:** China is the largest buyer, sourcing from regions where clearing destroyed USD 5 billion of annual soy production capacity (2013–2023). Germany’s imports are noted for being concentrated in high-clearing municipalities.

sources.title: Connecting Brazil’s Financial Market to Environmental Violations: The Hidden Impacts of Agribusiness on Indigenous Lands – Global Investigative Journalism Network
sources.content:
The provided text discusses an investigation into the Fiagro investment fund (Investment Funds in Agro-industrial Production Chains) and its links to environmental violations in the Brazilian Cerrado and Amazon biomes between March 2023 and March 2025.

### Deforestation and Environmental Threats in the Cerrado
*   **Context:** The Cerrado is described as the country’s “water tank,” supplying water to the entire country. It is currently threatened by the expansion of monoculture crops.
*   **Illegal Activities:** Investigations by 'O Joio e O Trigo' uncovered that Fiagro was financing illegal agribusiness activities within the Cerrado ecosystem.
*   **Commodity Drivers:** The main drivers of environmental degradation and land conflict mentioned include:
    *   **Monoculture crops:** Soy, corn, and cotton are identified as primary drivers, with farms operating on recognized Indigenous lands (specifically Kawaiweté/Kayabi territories in Mato Grosso).
    *   **Cattle:** The investigation tracked cattle raised in embargoed areas (overlapping Tanaru Indigenous land) which were then "laundered" through legal farms and sold to major meatpackers like JBS and Minerva Foods.
    *   **Biofuels:** Shareholders in biofuels companies were found to be profiting from Fiagro assets while being linked to land conflicts.

### IBAMA Enforcement Actions and Data
*   **Role of IBAMA:** The Brazilian Institute of Environment and Renewable Natural Resources (Ibama) is the regulatory agency responsible for issuing **embargoes, notes of infraction, and fines**.
*   **Enforcement Actions:** 
    *   The investigation used IBAMA's official database to identify environmental crimes including specific terms of embargoes and notes of infraction.
    *   **Embargoes:** All commercial activities are legally prohibited in area's under IBAMA embargo. The report highlights violations where cattle were raised inside these prohibited zones.
    *   **Data Availability:** IBAMA maintains an open data portal (Integrated Information System) providing details on environmental infractions and fines, though the text notes that specific details on where capital market money ends up (the credit recipients) are often not publicly required from issuers.

### Financial Context (March 2023 – March 2025)
*   The Fiagro fund reached a net worth of **US$8.1 billion** on the Brazilian Stock Exchange (B3) during this two-year period.
*   Agribusiness Receivable Certificates (CRA) increased in volume by **46%**, reaching **US$28.3 billion**.
*   The report notes a lack of stringent sustainable rules or regulations from the CVM (Brazilian Securities and Exchange Commission) to prevent farmers engaged in illegal activities from accessing these funds.

sources.title: The Liability of Financial Institutions in Brazil for Environmental Violations: Reflections from Operation Caixa-Forte
sources.content:
On March 19, 2025, the Brazilian Institute of Environment and Renewable Natural Resources (IBAMA) launched Operation Caixa-Forte, a significant enforcement action targeting the financial sector's role in Cerrado deforestation. 

Key details of Operation Caixa-Forte:
- Objective: To inspect and penalize financial institutions that granted rural credit to properties already embargoed for illegal deforestation in the Cerrado biome.
- Current Enforcement Stats: IBAMA has fined three banks located in the states of Maranhão, Tocantins, and Piauí (key states in the Matopiba agricultural frontier). 
- Financial Penalties: Total fines issued so far exceed R$ 3.63 million.
- Potential Sanctions: Beyond fines, institutions face potential suspension or prohibition of new financing operations and the cancellation of environmental licenses. They may also face sanctions from the Central Bank of Brazil for non-compliance with financial regulations.
- Regulatory Context: The operation enforces the rule that rural credit cannot be granted to business located on properties with active environmental embargos resulting from illegal deforestation, as per Complementary Law No. 140/2011 and the Environmental Infractions and Embargoes Register.
- Data Sources: IBAMA utilizes databases including rural property records (CAR), vegetation suppression authorizations, and rural credit operation data to identify violations.

While the source focuses on financial liability, it highlights that the primary driver of this illegal deforestation in the Cerrado is the expansion of rural properties and agricultural activities supported by 'rural credit.' This points to the agri-commodity sector (typically soy and cattle in these specific states) as the underlying driver of the land clearing being targeted. The operation specifically targets the Matopiba region (Maranhão, Tocantins, and Piauí), which is the current frontier for Cerrado conversion.

query: What is the current deforestation rate in the Brazilian Cerrado biome, what are the main commodity drivers, and what specific enforcement actions has IBAMA taken in the last two years?
synthesis:
The most current official annual deforestation rate for the Brazilian Cerrado is **7,235 km² in 2025** (preliminary PRODES estimate, covering the period used by PRODES and released on **October 30, 2025**), which was an **11.49% decrease from 2024**. INPE states this continued the slowdown that began in 2024, after prior years of growth. The states with the largest cleared area in 2025 were **Maranhão (2,006 km²), Tocantins (1,489 km²), and Piauí (1,350 km²)**, and earlier federal reporting emphasized that most Cerrado clearing is concentrated in **MATOPIBA**. [Dados do Prodes apontam redução no desmatamento na Amazônia e no Cerrado brasileiros](https://www.gov.br/inpe/pt-br/assuntos/ultimas-noticias/dados-do-prodes-apontam-reducao-no-desmatamento-na-amazonia-e-no-cerrado-brasileiros-1) [Em 2025, desmatamento tem redução de 11,08% na Amazônia e 11,49% no Cerrado](https://www.gov.br/ibama/pt-br/assuntos/noticias/2025/em-2025-desmatamento-tem-reducao-de-11-08-na-amazonia-e-11-49-no-cerrado)

For context, Brazil’s updated NDC says the Cerrado’s **2024 deforestation rate fell 25.7% versus 2023**, and it links that reversal to PPCerrado implementation and tougher enforcement. The same NDC reports that from **August 2023 to July 2024**, IBAMA registered **733 infraction notices, 449 embargoes, and 124 seizure notices** in the Cerrado, with **more than R$225 million in fines** collected during that period. [BRAZIL’S NDC](https://www.gov.br/mma/pt-br/assuntos/mudanca-do-clima/NDC/contribuicao-nacionalmente-determinada-2024-2013-en)

On the **main commodity drivers**, the strongest overlap across the sources is that **soy and cattle/beef are the dominant commodity-linked drivers of Cerrado conversion/deforestation**. An IPEA study on the EU deforestation regulation says Cerrado deforestation is strongly linked to agricultural expansion, especially **soy and beef**, and finds impacts from the regulation would be more pronounced for **soy in the Cerrado** than for beef there. [Efeitos da lei europeia contra desmatamento sobre as exportações brasileiras](https://repositorio.ipea.gov.br/items/41549cf6-3f8c-4678-8ffa-ca873fdc5a8b) Global Canopy states that in Brazil the **cattle supply chain, combined with agriculture such as soy production, is responsible for up to 97% of deforestation and land conversion**, and specifically warns of deforestation shifting into the Cerrado. [Roadmap – Deforestation-Free Finance](https://guidance.globalcanopy.org/roadmap/) A Goiás state environmental planning document for the Cerrado identifies the biome’s main deforestation vectors as **grain production, sugarcane, cattle ranching, and charcoal for the steel industry**; it also notes the northward expansion of grain production, especially soy, in Maranhão, Piauí, Tocantins, and western Bahia. [Plano de Manejo da Estação Ecológica Chapada de Nova Roma – ENCARTE 1](https://goias.gov.br/meioambiente/wp-content/uploads/sites/33/2017/08/encarte-1-revisado-724.pdf)

So, if you want the commodity picture in one line: **soy and cattle are the primary commodity drivers**, with **other important but secondary drivers including grain expansion more broadly, sugarcane in some regions, and charcoal linked to pig iron/steel production**. [Efeitos da lei europeia contra desmatamento sobre as exportações brasileiras](https://repositorio.ipea.gov.br/items/41549cf6-3f8c-4678-8ffa-ca873fdc5a8b) [Roadmap – Deforestation-Free Finance](https://guidance.globalcanopy.org/roadmap/) [Plano de Manejo da Estação Ecológica Chapada de Nova Roma – ENCARTE 1](https://goias.gov.br/meioambiente/wp-content/uploads/sites/33/2017/08/encarte-1-revisado-724.pdf)

For **specific IBAMA enforcement actions in the last two years (2024–2025)**, I found several concrete measures and operations:

1. **Operation Nova Fronteira in MATOPIBA (October–November 2024):** IBAMA says this anti-deforestation operation in the Cerrado resulted in **52 administrative violation notices**, about **R$206 million in environmental fines**, and the **seizure of 23 pieces of equipment and machinery** such as tractors and sprayers. IBAMA said satellite image analysis showed that prior **embargoes had been violated**, and offenders had been profiting from **soy, corn, bean and other crop production**, including sale and leasing of embargoed land. [Desmatamento ilegal no Cerrado gera R$ 206 milhões em multas por descumprimento de embargos e impedimento da regeneracao natural](https://www.gov.br/ibama/pt-br/assuntos/noticias/2024/desmatamento-ilegal-no-cerrado-gera-r-206-milhoes-em-multas-por-descumprimento-de-embargos-e-impedimento-da-regeneracao-natural)

2. **Broad Cerrado enforcement escalation in 2023–2024, reported in September 2024:** On September 11, 2024, IBAMA said that in **2023 and 2024** it had issued **2,768 infraction notices for illegal deforestation in the Cerrado**. The same note says at least **half of Cerrado deforestation is estimated to occur without authorization**, framing these sanctions as part of permanent enforcement aligned with PPCerrado. [No Dia Nacional do Cerrado, Ibama combate incêndios florestais e desmatamento ilegal no bioma](https://www.gov.br/ibama/pt-br/assuntos/noticias/2024/no-dia-nacional-do-cerrado-ibama-combate-incendios-florestais-e-desmatamento-ilegal-no-bioma)

3. **Remote embargoes and stepped-up enforcement thresholds:** Brazil’s NDC says IBAMA increased the use of **remote embargoes** in the Cerrado for areas with **illegal deforestation above 0.5 km² (50 hectares)**, compared with an average biome event size of **5 km² (500 hectares)**. Over **August 2023–July 2024**, the NDC attributes to IBAMA **733 infraction notices, 449 embargoes, 124 seizure notices, and more than R$225 million in fines** in the Cerrado. [BRAZIL’S NDC](https://www.gov.br/mma/pt-br/assuntos/mudanca-do-clima/NDC/contribuicao-nacionalmente-determinada-2024-2013-en)

4. **Cerrado enforcement totals through May 2025:** IBAMA reported that from **August 2024 to May 2025** in the Cerrado it had imposed **R$578 million in fines**, issued **737 infraction notices** and **455 embargoes**, plus **376 seizure notices** covering **R$54.9 million in seized goods**, **77 destruction notices**, **370 fire-prevention notifications**, and **20 civil public actions**. [No mês de maio, Pantanal e Cerrado registram continuidade na queda no desmatamento; Amazônia é afetada por incêndios e seca histórica](https://www.gov.br/ibama/pt-br/assuntos/noticias/2025/no-mes-de-maio-pantanal-e-cerrado-registram-continuidade-na-queda-no-desmatamento-amazonia-e-afetada-por-incendios-e-seca-historica)

5. **Triângulo Mineiro operation (May 2025):** IBAMA says an operation concluded on **May 16, 2025** in **Uberlândia and Uberaba, Minas Gerais**, found **500 hectares of illegally cleared protected areas**, which were **embargoed**, and generated **more than R$4.2 million in fines**. Inspectors used **satellite imagery and drones**, found illegal conversion of legal reserves and permanent protection areas into **pasture, silviculture, and agriculture**, and identified drainage works in wetlands. [Desmatamento ilegal gera mais de R$ 4,2 milhões em multas no Triângulo Mineiro](https://www.gov.br/ibama/pt-br/assuntos/noticias/2025/desmatamento-ilegal-gera-mais-de-r-4-2-milhoes-em-multas-no-triangulo-mineiro)

A few caveats on the evidence base: the **official current deforestation rate** is available from **INPE/PRODES** and is the strongest source for the first part of your question. The **commodity-driver** literature is more heterogeneous: official and policy sources consistently point to **soy and cattle**, while one regional planning document adds **sugarcane and charcoal** as important vectors; I did not find a single recent federal source that ranked all Cerrado commodities quantitatively in one table. For **IBAMA actions**, the clearest recent evidence comes from **IBAMA’s own releases** plus Brazil’s **2024 NDC**, but the figures refer to somewhat different time windows, so they should be read as complementary rather than directly additive. [Dados do Prodes apontam redução no desmatamento na Amazônia e no Cerrado brasileiros](https://www.gov.br/inpe/pt-br/assuntos/ultimas-noticias/dados-do-prodes-apontam-reducao-no-desmatamento-na-amazonia-e-no-cerrado-brasileiros-1) [BRAZIL’S NDC](https://www.gov.br/mma/pt-br/assuntos/mudanca-do-clima/NDC/contribuicao-nacionalmente-determinada-2024-2013-en) [Desmatamento ilegal no Cerrado gera R$ 206 milhões em multas por descumprimento de embargos e impedimento da regeneracao natural](https://www.gov.br/ibama/pt-br/assuntos/noticias/2024/desmatamento-ilegal-no-cerrado-gera-r-206-milhoes-em-multas-por-descumprimento-de-embargos-e-impedimento-da-regeneracao-natural)

If useful, I can next turn this into a **briefing table** with columns for **rate / driver / enforcement action / date / source**.

sources.title: Dados do Prodes apontam redução no desmatamento na Amazônia e no Cerrado brasileiros
sources.content: Published 30/10/2025. Official INPE/PRODES release. States that the Cerrado estimated deforestation rate for 2025 was 7,235 km², a reduction of 11.49% compared with the previous year. Says this consolidates a trend of deceleration after years of growth and a reversal that began in 2024. Identifies states with the largest area of native vegetation suppressed in 2025: Maranhão 2,006 km², Tocantins 1,489 km², Piauí 1,350 km². Explains PRODES is INPE’s annual official monitoring system for suppression of native vegetation in Brazilian biomes. Notes the values released are preliminary estimates for 2025, with consolidated rate to be published in first half of 2026.

sources.title: Em 2025, desmatamento tem redução de 11,08% na Amazônia e 11,49% no Cerrado
sources.content: IBAMA release on 2025 PRODES results. Reports 11.49% decline in Cerrado deforestation in 2025 and says 77.9% of deforestation in the biome occurred in Maranhão, Tocantins, Piauí and Bahia (MATOPIBA). Notes this was the third consecutive year of reduction since the start of the Lula administration and that the accumulated decline in the biome in 2025 versus 2022 reached 50%. Mentions PRODES supports enforcement and control actions by IBAMA and ICMBio.

sources.title: BRAZIL’S NDC
sources.content: Brazil’s updated NDC, opened as PDF. In section PPCerrado, states that in 2024 Brazil achieved a 25.7% reduction in deforestation rates in the Brazilian Cerrado compared to 2023. Attributes reversal to actions including launch of PPCerrado in November 2023; increase in remote embargoes by IBAMA for areas with illegal deforestation above 0.5 km² (50 hectares), compared with an average for the biome of 5 km² (500 hectares); intensified inspection. Specifies that from August 2023 to July 2024, IBAMA registered 733 infraction notices, 449 embargoes, and 124 seizure notices in the Cerrado, with more than R$225 million in fines during the period. Also mentions creation of a task force with ministers and governors of Cerrado states, involvement of agribusiness leaders, more transparency and integration of ASV data, and stricter prohibition of rural credit in illegally deforested areas across biomes.

sources.title: Efeitos da lei europeia contra desmatamento sobre as exportações brasileiras
sources.content: IPEA repository page for a study on the EU Deforestation Regulation’s effects on Brazilian exports. States deforestation is strongly linked to agricultural expansion, especially soy and beef production and other forest-risk commodities. Says impacts of the regulation would be more pronounced for soy in the Cerrado and for beef in the Amazon. Reports the methodology identified 480 critical municipalities for soy and 918 for beef. Says critical exports potentially affected equal 7% of the total in the Amazon and 3% in the Cerrado. Emphasizes need for better traceability and field studies to link agricultural inputs to deforestation more robustly.

sources.title: Roadmap – Deforestation-Free Finance
sources.content: Global Canopy guidance page. States cattle is the biggest driver of deforestation and conversion globally. Says Brazil accounts for nearly 60% of global pasture-driven deforestation. Adds that within Brazil, the cattle supply chain, combined with agriculture such as soy production, is responsible for up to 97% of deforestation and land conversion. Notes deforestation is shifting from ecosystems like the Amazon to regions such as the Cerrado.

sources.title: Plano de Manejo da Estação Ecológica Chapada de Nova Roma – ENCARTE 1
sources.content: PDF from Goiás environmental authorities. Relevant lines say current occupation trend is in the northern Cerrado, with clearing for grain production especially in southern Maranhão, Piauí, and northern Tocantins, and rapid soy expansion in western Bahia. States the main vectors for deforestation in the Cerrado biome are grain production, sugarcane, cattle ranching, and charcoal for supplying steel industries. Also mentions mining, silviculture, and forest fires as additional factors causing deforestation and negative impacts. Historical land-use figures in the document say of total deforested Cerrado by 2002, 54 million hectares (26.5%) were occupied by cultivated pastures and 21.56 million hectares (10.5%) by agricultural crops.

sources.title: Desmatamento ilegal no Cerrado gera R$ 206 milhões em multas por descumprimento de embargos e impedimento da regeneracao natural
sources.content: IBAMA news release published 02/12/2024. Describes Operation Nova Fronteira in the Cerrado in MATOPIBA (Maranhão, Tocantins, Piauí, Bahia). Reports 52 autuações (administrative violation notices) totaling approximately R$206 million in environmental fines during October and November 2024. Says 23 pieces of equipment and machinery, including tractors and sprayers, were seized. Satellite geospatial analysis showed prior embargoes on inspected areas had not been respected. According to the inspection, offenders had for years been generating illicit economic gains from sale of soybeans, corn, beans and other crops, as well as sale and leasing of embargoed lands.

sources.title: No Dia Nacional do Cerrado, Ibama combate incêndios florestais e desmatamento ilegal no bioma
sources.content: IBAMA news release published 11/09/2024. States PPCerrado estimates at least half of deforestation in the Cerrado occurs without authorization. Says IBAMA carries out permanent inspection actions in the biome aligned with PPCerrado and that in 2023 and 2024 the Institute issued 2,768 infraction notices for illegal deforestation in the Cerrado. Also provides fire context: 54,200 heat spots since start of 2024 at the time of publication; 436 brigadistas operating in the biome with five airplanes and two helicopters; 349 fires recorded in the Cerrado up to 9 September, of which 188 active, 161 extinguished, and 65 controlled.

sources.title: No mês de maio, Pantanal e Cerrado registram continuidade na queda no desmatamento; Amazônia é afetada por incêndios e seca histórica
sources.content: IBAMA release published in 2025. In the enforcement section, says that in the Cerrado from August 2024 to May 2025 IBAMA applied R$578 million in fines, issued 737 infraction notices and 455 embargoes. Also says 376 seizure notices were issued, totaling R$54.9 million in seized goods, along with 77 destruction notices, 370 notifications for preventive fire measures, and 20 civil public actions. Framed as part of intensified federal enforcement and accountability against illegal deforestation.

sources.title: Desmatamento ilegal gera mais de R$ 4,2 milhões em multas no Triângulo Mineiro
sources.content: IBAMA release published 20/05/2025. Reports an operation in Uberlândia and Uberaba, Minas Gerais, concluded on 16 May 2025. Resulted in more than R$4.2 million in environmental fines related to 500 hectares of illegally deforested protected areas, which were embargoed. Inspectors used satellite images and drones and found up to 14% of legal reserve and permanent preservation areas had been irregularly converted into pasture, silviculture and agriculture, preventing regeneration of native vegetation. Also identified drainage channels in wetlands. Notes that in the Cerrado outside the Legal Amazon, the 2012 Forest Code requires rural properties to maintain at least 20% native vegetation.
